summary: This is a very brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's monthly Inc. magazine column. The core advice is about not imposing startup-era frugality on your successful employees. Joel argues that as a company grows, you should let star performers travel and work in comfort rather than forcing the scrappy cost-cutting that founders endured in the early days. The implication is that penny-pinching on employee perks damages morale and retention.
key_points: Company spending norms should evolve as the business matures beyond the bootstrap phase
Imposing founder-era austerity on star employees signals a lack of respect for their contributions
Employee morale is connected to how generously and professionally they are treated in their work conditions
money_quotes: Don't tell your star salespeople to take the bus and stay with relatives when they make that call in St. Louis, even though that's what you did when you started the company.
key_insight: As your company grows past the bootstrap phase, clinging to founder-era frugality with your top performers undermines morale and signals you don't value them.
